name: Sahelanthropus tchadensis
description: One of the oldest known species in the human family tree, Sahelanthropus tchadensis lived around 7 million years ago in Central Africa. Discovered in Chad in 2001, this species shows a mix of ape-like and human-like features. The braincase is small, like a chimpanzee, but the face is relatively flat and the canine teeth are smaller than those of other apes.

name: Australopithecus afarensis
description: Australopithecus afarensis, best known from the famous skeleton 'Lucy', lived in eastern Africa between 3.9 and 2.9 million years ago. This species walked upright on two legs but still had features well-suited for climbing trees. Their brain was about the size of a chimpanzee's, and they had protruding faces with large canine teeth relative to modern humans.

name: Homo habilis
description: Homo habilis, meaning 'handy man', is one of the earliest members of our genus Homo. Living in Africa between 2.4 and 1.4 million years ago, H. habilis made and used simple stone tools known as Oldowan tools — sharp flakes struck from stone cores. This species had a significantly larger brain than earlier australopiths and a more human-like hand capable of fine manipulation.

name: Homo erectus
description: Homo erectus was the first human species to spread out of Africa into Asia and Europe. With a brain roughly 70–80% the size of a modern human's, H. erectus was an accomplished hunter and tool-maker. They created the Acheulean hand axe, built fires, and likely developed simple social structures. Fossils have been found from Africa to Indonesia ('Java Man') to China ('Peking Man').

name: Homo heidelbergensis
description: Homo heidelbergensis is considered an intermediate species between Homo erectus and both Neanderthals and modern humans. This species had a large brain approaching modern human size, hunted large animals using wooden spears, and may have built shelters. Found in Africa and Europe, H. heidelbergensis represents the last common ancestor of Neanderthals and Homo sapiens.

name: Homo neanderthalensis
description: Neanderthals were our closest evolutionary relatives, living in Europe and western Asia until about 40,000 years ago. They had large brains (sometimes even larger than modern humans), stocky cold-adapted bodies, and sophisticated tool cultures (Mousterian technology). Recent DNA analysis shows that modern humans of non-African descent carry 1–4% Neanderthal DNA, evidence of interbreeding. Neanderthals buried their dead and may have practiced rudimentary art.

traits.diet: High proportion of meat, some plants

name: Denisovans
description: Denisovans are an extinct group of archaic humans discovered through ancient DNA analysis of a finger bone and teeth found in Denisova Cave, Siberia. Genetically distinct from both modern humans and Neanderthals, Denisovans interbred with the ancestors of modern Melanesians, Aboriginal Australians, and some Southeast Asian populations, who carry up to 5% Denisovan DNA. Very little skeletal material is known, making them one of the most enigmatic human relatives.

name: Homo floresiensis
description: Nicknamed 'The Hobbit', Homo floresiensis was a remarkably small-bodied human relative discovered on the Indonesian island of Flores. Standing only about 1 meter tall with a brain the size of a grapefruit, H. floresiensis nonetheless made stone tools and hunted small elephants. Their small size may have resulted from island dwarfism. This species challenges our understanding of hominin diversity in Asia.

traits.diet: Small game, plants, possibly Stegodon (pygmy elephant)

name: Homo sapiens
description: Homo sapiens — modern humans — emerged in Africa approximately 300,000 years ago. We are characterized by our large, globular brains, our capacity for complex language and symbolic thought, and our extraordinary cultural diversity. Modern humans spread out of Africa around 60,000–70,000 years ago, eventually colonizing every continent. Our species has fundamentally transformed the planet through agriculture, cities, technology, and art.

traits.diet: Highly varied omnivorous diet; agriculture from ~10,000 ya
traits.tools: All tool technologies; written language, computers
